source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 16.2.2 Log rotation (pp. 308-313)

questions.q: What three things does log rotation do?
options: Rotate (archive current log + start a fresh one), compress old archives, and delete outdated ones
questions.why: Rotation archives and restarts the log, compresses old ones, and deletes those past the retention policy - preventing unbounded growth.

questions.q: With the 'dateext' parameter, how is a rotated 'secure' log named?
questions.why: dateext appends a date (secure-YYYYMMDD); without it, rotations are numbered (secure.1, secure.2, ...).

questions.q: What does the 'rotate 4' directive mean in /etc/logrotate.conf?
options: Keep 4 rotated files before deleting the oldest
questions.why: rotate <N> keeps N old files after rotation; older ones are removed.

questions.q: Which directory holds per-application logrotate configuration snippets?
questions.why: /etc/logrotate.conf is the global config; /etc/logrotate.d/ holds the per-package snippets (included via 'include').

questions.q: Run logrotate against /etc/logrotate.conf verbosely to see what it does.
questions.why: -v shows the rotation process; -f forces rotation even when the conditions aren't met.

options: Skips rotation if the log file is empty
Emails the admin when the log is empty
questions.why: notifempty prevents rotating an empty log; missingok ignores a missing log without warning.
